First tip is to use clear instructions. So, if you want an LM to summarize a paper for you, instead of writing a prompt like this, write something with more details. So a very common way this is implemented is we'll start with some user query like what is rag but instead of just passing this query directly to a large language model, we'll first have this retrieval step where we'll take that query and do some kind of search over a knowledge base or even over the internet. Then we can pass this prompt to a large language model and then we can generate a response. You can think of fine-tuning as the following process where we have this pre-trained model which is kind of like a blank slab of marble. Fine-tuning is the process of chiseling and refining the slab of marble into something more suitable for our specific use case. But often you don't just fine-tune a model one time. You can fine-tune a model multiple times to continuously refine it to a specific use case. And so this is exactly what OpenAI did when they created the initial version of chatbt. They had this pre-trained base model called GPT3 and then they taught it to follow instructions through a supervised fine-tuning process and then they further aligned it with human preferences through this technique called reinforcement learning with human feedback and this is what resulted in their final model of instruct GPT which you can read more about in reference number one but basically this was the precursor to chat GPT and what ultimately led to this explosion of AI we're seeing Today, the key benefit of fine-tuning is that fine-tuned models can often outperform bigger ones on specific tasks. So, instruct GPT is actually a great example of this in which a model with just 1 billion parameters outperformed the base model GPT3, which was over a 100 times bigger as assessed by like human preferences. So, humans significantly preferred instruct GPT's responses to GPT3. But some situations where fine-tuning may be helpful is if you want to give the model a unique personality, when you want to customize the response format, like maybe you want it to be in like a specific XML format or you want to teach it a new programming language. When you're trying to get the model to do tasks that are easier to show rather than tell. Basically, you'll have a teacher model and you'll use that teacher model to generate data and then you'll use that data to train or even fine-tune a student model.
